coredata.dc:title: Machine learning-assisted three-dimensional fluorescence for heavy metal multi-sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiao
Xiong, Xiaolong
Ye, Jingru
Shuai, Xinjia
Zhou, Jun
Liu, Qingqing
Huang, Chengzhi
Liu, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal pollution is pervasive, complex, and diverse, causing lasting and potentially toxic effects on human health. Therefore, it is necessary to develop high-throughput detection of heavy metals. In this work, we proposed a machine learning-based three-dimensional fluorescent sensor strategy for effective detection of heavy metal pollution. Specifically, a multi-emitting fluorescent "chemical nose/tongue" sensor was first constructed with two different colored CDs and 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-trimethylamino) phenyl porphyrin (TAPP) for discriminating nine heavy metals, and then the fluorescence fingerprints were obtained based on the changes in the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra influenced by various heavy metals. The results demonstrated that the sensor easily and rapidly discriminated the categories and concentrations of multiple single heavy metal ions and binary mixed heavy metal ions within the concentration range of 10 μM–0.5 mM. Subsequently, the data input way was optimized, and the results showed that the accuracy of image recognition in predicting categories and concentrations of heavy metal ions was 75.1 % and 64.4 %, and the accuracy of data recognition was 97.8 % and 100 %. After optimizing the learning model, the Random Forest (RF) model can predict the categories and concentrations of a binary heavy metal mixture with accuracies of 100 % and 91.8 %, respectively. In addition, machine learning-assisted fluorescent sensors showed promising applications in real tap water and river samples. In conclusion, compared to previous analysis methods, this approach achieved qualitative and quantitative analysis of heavy metals in complex environments through three-dimensional fluorescence spectra combined with the ML strategy. Notably, the sensor did not involve cumbersome synthesis of multiple sensing channels, making it easy to construct and cost-effective.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heavy metals contamination
Machine learning
Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra
Multi-color carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The construction of carbon dot/TAPP fluorescence sensor
Fluorescence sensing for differentiation of heavy metal ions
Fluorescence sensing for distinction of binary heavy metal ion mixtures
Machine learning model building and optimization
Construction and optimization of carbon dot/TAPP fluorescence sensor
Multi-sensing of nine heavy metal ions
Multi-sensing of binary heavy metal ion mixtures
Machine learning model building and optimization
Categories and concentrations prediction of heavy metal ions by machine learning models

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing Municipality
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Chengzhi Huang is a Ph.D. holder and doctoral supervisor specializing in pharmaceutical analysis. He is a recipient of the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars, a selected candidate of the National New Century Talents Project, and a Chongqing Talents Program Outstanding Scientist. His research focuses on drug spectral analysis, including biocomputing, intelligent drug analysis, energy transfer spectral analysis, nanoassemblies and bioimaging, and real-time analysis of virus-infected cells.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 22274134, 21705132, 22376169) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1519).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectrum of (a) B-CDs and (b)Y-CDs. UV-absorption visible spectra and fluorescence spectra of (c) B-CDs with (d) Y-CDs. (Inset: Photographs of B-CDs and Y-CDs solutions under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp irradiation). The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of (e) B-CDs and (f) Y-CDs. (The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of B-CDs were 365 nm and 459 nm, respectively, and those of Y-CDs were 429 nm and 568 nm, respectively.).

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectrum and (b) the fluorescence spectrum of B-CDs+Y-CDs, TAPP, and B-CDs+Y-CDs+TAPP. Three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of (c) B-CDs+Y-CDs and (d) B-CDs+Y-CDs+TAPP. (The excitation wavelength ranges from 300 to 600 nm, and the emission wavelength ranges from 300 to 800 nm). Clustering analyses of sensor arrays discriminating metal ions at pH (e) 4.00 and (f) 6.09. (After principal component analysis, the data of five repeated experiments of all heavy metal ions did not cross and did not overlap., which proved that the sensor could separate these heavy metal ions.).

The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the sensor array after addition of (a) 10 μM, (b) 50 μM, (c) 100 μM, and (d) 200 μM of Ni2+. (e) The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the sensor array after addition of 100 μM of the binary mixture of Cd2++As3+ with a concentration ratio of 3:2 and (f) the binary mixture of Pb2++Cd2+ with a concentration ratio of 4:1. (The discrimination of single heavy metal ion and binary mixed heavy metal ion was carried out at 37 °C for 30 minutes.).

(a) The process of building the machine learning model. Predictions of the Random Forest model for the (b) categories (From 1–9 are Ni2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Zn2+, As3+, As5+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, respectively) and (c) concentrations (From 1–5 are 10 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 500 μM, respectively) of single heavy metal ion. Predictions of the Random Forest model for (d) categories (From 1–4 are Pb2+/Cd2+, Cd2+/As3+, As3+/Cr3+, and Cr3+/Pb2+(The molar ratio was 3:2 and the total concentration was fixed at 100 μM)). and (e) concentrations of binary mixed heavy metal ions. (From 1–4 are Pb2+ and Cd2+ with molar ratios of 4:1, 3:2, 2:3, and 1:4 (the total concentration was fixed at 100 μM)).

(a) Schematic diagram of the fluorescent sensor array used to differentiate between metal ions in tap water and river. The RF model predicted a single heavy metal ion (b) categories in the real sample river and tap water ((From 1–9 are Ni2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Zn2+, As3+, As5+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, respectively)). The RF model predicted a binary mixture of heavy metal ions (c) category (From 1–4 are Pb2+/Cd2+, Cd2+/As3+, As3+/Cr3+, and Cr3+/Pb2+(The molar ratio was 3:2 and the total concentration was fixed at 100 μM)) and (d) concentrations (From 1–4 are Pb2+ and Cd2+ with molar ratios of 4:1, 3:2, 2:3, and 1:4 (the total concentration was fixed at 100 μM)) in the real sample river and tap water.

Machine learning-guided "chemical nasal tongue" sensor for heavy metal ions. (a) Preparation of a fluorescent sensor to build a dataset for machine learning. (b) Machine learning model with fluorescence fingerprinting of heavy metal ions as input and concentration and class prediction as output.

head.ce:title: Machine learning-assisted three-dimensional fluorescence for heavy metal multi-sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Resources and Environment, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Resources and Environment, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Resources and Environment, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal pollution is pervasive, complex, and diverse, causing lasting and potentially toxic effects on human health. Therefore, it is necessary to develop high-throughput detection of heavy metals. In this work, we proposed a machine learning-based three-dimensional fluorescent sensor strategy for effective detection of heavy metal pollution. Specifically, a multi-emitting fluorescent "chemical nose/tongue" sensor was first constructed with two different colored CDs and 5,10,15,20-tetrakis(4-trimethylamino) phenyl porphyrin (TAPP) for discriminating nine heavy metals, and then the fluorescence fingerprints were obtained based on the changes in the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra influenced by various heavy metals. The results demonstrated that the sensor easily and rapidly discriminated the categories and concentrations of multiple single heavy metal ions and binary mixed heavy metal ions within the concentration range of 10 μM–0.5 mM. Subsequently, the data input way was optimized, and the results showed that the accuracy of image recognition in predicting categories and concentrations of heavy metal ions was 75.1 % and 64.4 %, and the accuracy of data recognition was 97.8 % and 100 %. After optimizing the learning model, the Random Forest (RF) model can predict the categories and concentrations of a binary heavy metal mixture with accuracies of 100 % and 91.8 %, respectively. In addition, machine learning-assisted fluorescent sensors showed promising applications in real tap water and river samples. In conclusion, compared to previous analysis methods, this approach achieved qualitative and quantitative analysis of heavy metals in complex environments through three-dimensional fluorescence spectra combined with the ML strategy. Notably, the sensor did not involve cumbersome synthesis of multiple sensing channels, making it easy to construct and cost-effective.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals in the environment can enter the food chain, causing irreversible damage to human health.[1,2] Therefore, it is of great significance to detect various heavy metals. The "chemical nose/tongue" strategy is a method to identify analytes based on the fingerprints generated by the interactions between the array and multiple analytes.[3–5] Currently, this strategy has been widely used to construct various chemical or biological sensor arrays to detect multiple targets.[6–8] The fluorescence analysis method has been applied to the construction of sensor arrays because of its fast reaction speed, high detection sensitivity, and simple operation.[9,10]

For the fluorescence sensor array, the choice of fluorescent materials and number of fluorescent channels are very important for the effect of the sensor array to distinguish a variety of heavy metal ions.[11,12] Carbon dots (CDs), as one of the most important and attractive fluorescent nanomaterials, have the advantages of wide source of raw materials, convenient preparation, and excellent photostability,[13–18] which make them an ideal candidate for constructing fluorescent sensor.[19,20] Additionally, TAPP has a special rigid π-electron domain structure,[21] which can form supramolecular complexes with the surface of CDs through π-π or charge-transfer interactions.[22–24] In this way, the fluorescence emission intensity of CDs is quenched or enhanced. Based on this change in fluorescence signal, the sensor can detect various analytes with greater sensitivity. In addition, the four nitrogen atoms can form stable complexes with metal ions. For meso-Tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin (TCPP), the four -NH2 on the benzene ring of TAPP is an electron-donating group, which enhances the conjugation system of the sensor, thus contributing to the stability to the fluorescence intensity of the system.[25] Furthermore, although the fewer sensor elements in a sensor array, the less data it produces, the easier it is to construct subsequent methods of data analysis[26]. However, the fluorescence signal collected in the experiment was the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra. The higher the data abundance, the better the detection effect of the array. Therefore, we used TAPP as a complexing agent with metal ions, and two different colors of carbon dots as the main signal, so as to construct a sensor with three signal channels. After the addition of metal ions, the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots was affected by TAPP, and finally the purpose of distinguishing was achieved.

Sensor arrays usually consist of multiple sensing elements that generate a large amount of high-dimensional and complex data in the process of identifying targets.[27] Therefore, it is particularly important to choose appropriate and efficient methods for processing the data.[28] Currently, Machine Learning (ML) is uniquely suited to deal with massive amounts of data and has a broad application in fields such as chemistry, medicine and materials.[29–31] Various methods such as principal component analysis (PCA), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), and convolutional neural networks (CNN) have been used to analyse data.[32–34] Xie and coworkers provided a strategy that used UV-Vis absorption spectra combined with PCA to achieve nine kinds of metal ion detection.[35] The method is simple and feasible, and can distinguish metal ions only by a single probe P combined with PCA. However, due to the abundant information of the three-dimensional fluorescence spectral data involved in our experiment, PCA dimension reduction may lead to the loss of some key spectral features or material information, which may affect the subsequent analysis and application.[36] In addition, there may be some noise signals or outliers, which will have a great impact on the calculation results of PCA, resulting in the principal component cannot accurately reflect the essential characteristics of the data.

The input data for ML models can affect the performance of model predictions and the detection performance of the sensor. It is extremely important to establish appropriate fluorescence data acquisition methods and learning model data input ways. Among them, the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra containing comprehensive and rich information that can fully describe the change of image characteristics of fluorescent substances are used to increase the differentiation of the sensor.[37,38] For example, Cui's group proposed deep learning-assisted three-dimensional fluorescence difference spectroscopy for fast and simple detection of five illicit drugs in biofluids.[39] Therefore, three-dimensional fluorescence spectra with the aid of ML models may yield accurate predictions even with small amounts of sample.

In this work, blue-emitting (B-CDs) and yellow-emitting (Y-CDs) CDs and TAPP were chosen to construct a multi-emitting fluorescence sensor for the qualitative and quantitative analysis of nine kinds of heavy metal ions with the help of ML strategy ( Scheme 1). Due to the good response between the heavy metal ions and the multi-emitting sensor, the fluorescence intensity changed with the addition of metal ions. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectral intensity changes after the addition of each heavy metal ion to the sensor were analyzed to obtain a fingerprint of the fluorescence analysis. The best model was optimized by introducing the ML strategy, and the model was used to further analyze and process the multidimensional data obtained from the sensor. It was demonstrated that the multi-emitting fluorescence sensor could easily and rapidly identify the categories and concentrations of multiple single heavy metal ions and binary mixed heavy metal ions in the concentration range of 10 μΜ–0.5 mM. The data obtained by the three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum can be input in two ways, which had a great impact on the prediction performance of the model, so we compared the prediction accuracy of the two input ways, image recognition and data recognition. The results indicated

that the accuracy of the image input method for predicting the categories and concentrations of heavy metal ions was 75.1 % and 64.4 %, respectively, while the prediction accuracy of the spectral data input method was 97.8 % and 100 %. Therefore, the method of data identification is utilized as the input method for ML. The optimal learning model, RF, achieved prediction accuracy of 100 % and 91.8 % for

the category and concentration of binary heavy metal mixtures, respectively. Finally, the average accuracy of predicting a single heavy metal category in the real sample river and tap water was 94.4 %. Overall, the ML strategy to screen and optimize the method can achieve the qualitative and quantitative analysis for heavy metal multi-sensing in complex environments. Notably, the sensor was easy and cost- effective to construct because it did not involve the tedious synthesis of multiple sensing channels.

Citric acid (CA), disodium hydrogen arsenate (Na2HAsO4), and lead nitrate (Pb (NO3)2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). o-Phenylenediamine (o-PD), nickel chloride (NiCl2), and polyethyleneimine (PEI, MW: 1800) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Cd (NO3)2) were purchased from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co. Copper sulfate (CuSO4) was purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co. Chromium chloride (CrCl3) and anhydrous ethanol (EtOH) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. Manganese sulfate (MnSO4) was purchased from Chongqing Chemical Reagent Factory. Ferric chloride (FeCl3), and zinc sulfate (ZnSO4) were purchased from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Co. Ferrous chloride (FeCl2) was purchased from Tianjin Bailens Biotechnology Co. Sodium arsenite (NaAsO2) was purchased from Beijing Inokai Technology Co. All reagents and solutions used in this study were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The water used for the experiments was ultrapure (18.2 MΩ∙cm−1, Milli-Q).

UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum were measured and analyzed by U-3010 spectrophotometer (Shanghai Techcomp Instrument Ltd.) and F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The morphology and lattice images of CDs were measured using the FEI Talos F200X field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI. America). The functional groups and elements of the CDs were accurately analyzed by using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer IRPrestige-21 (Shimadzu, Japan) and an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (USA). The Raman spectra of the CDs were measured by a LabRAM HR800 laser confocal Raman spectrometer (Horiba. France) was measured. The fluorescence quantum yields of CDs were measured by an absolute photoluminescence quantum yield meter C11347 (Hamamatsu, Japan). The zeta potential of the samples was measured by a dynamic light scattering particle sizer (ZEN3600, Malvern). Data processing was mainly performed by downscaling and clustering analysis using PyCharm Community Edition software, and then the data were plotted by Origin 2021 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The construction of carbon dot/TAPP fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: First, two different color CDs (20 μL) and TAPP (20 μL) were added into BR buffer (50 μL). The concentrations of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and TAPP were 0.2mg/mL, 0.2 mg/ML, and 6 μM, respectively, the volume was replenished with ultrapure water to 190 μL of the mixture, which was then transferred to 37 °C in Eppendorf ThermoMixer® C for 30 min. After the reaction, F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to obtain fluorescence spectra (I 0). When different heavy metal ions (10 μL) were introduced and incubated for 30 min, the final fluorescence intensity (I) and the changes in relative fluorescence intensity (I 0-I)/I 0 were recorded as a response signal for the sensing analysis. Each target was repeated through five parallel experiments. Finally, data was processed in Origin and Pycharm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing for differentiation of heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: B-CDs (0.2 mg/mL), Y-CDs (0.2 mg/mL), and TAPP (6 μM) were added to BR buffer (pH = 6.09), and 10, 50, 100, 200, and 500 μM of heavy metal ions (Ni2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Co2+, Zn2+, As3+, As5+, Cd2+, and Pb2+) were added to the sensor and reacted at 37 °C for 30 min. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were then detected with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The three-dimensional fingerprints of single heavy metal ions in a fluorescent sensor were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing for distinction of binary heavy metal ion mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The heavy metal ions Pb2+ and Cd2+ were selected to prepare binary mixtures with molar ratios of 4:1, 3:2, 2:3, and 1:4 (the total concentration was fixed at 100 μM), and the 100 μM binary mixed heavy metal ion solution was added to the sensor and reacted for 30 min. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were measured at the excitation wavelength in the range of 300–600 nm and the emission wavelength in the range of 300–800 nm by using an F-7000 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. The heavy metals (Cr3+, As3+, Cd2+, and Pb2+) were combined into a binary mixture with a molar ratio of 3:2 according to Pb2+/Cd2+, Cd2+/As3+, As3+/Cr3+, and Cr3+/Pb2+. The total concentration of the two metal ions was fixed at 100 μM and then added to the sensor solution at 37 °C for 30 min. Finally, record and save the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of different mixtures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Machine learning model building and optimization
ce:section.ce:para: Before building and optimizing the machine learning model, we first wrote the algorithm code of the learning model. The learning model was constructed using the data in the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra as input and the categories and concentrations of heavy metal ions as output. Firstly, the datasets were divided into training and test sets in the ratio of 8:2. Next, the performance of different learning models was compared to improve the learning efficiency, namely, random forest (RF), support vector machine (SVM), logistic regression, artificial neural network (ANN), decision tree (DT), and tested their performance to select the best learning model during the iterative training of ML models. Initially, the category prediction model for heavy metal ions was optimized. Mean absolute error (MAE) and coefficient of determination (R 2) were commonly used to evaluate the performance of the model, along with accuracy and mean square error (MSE) were used to measure the performance of the learning model. [40–42]

The real sample solutions containing different concentrations of single heavy metal ions and binary mixed heavy metal ions were added to the sensor and reacted for 30 min at 37 °C. At the end of the reaction, the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the reaction solution were measured.

After the synthesis of the B-CDs and Y-CDs by one-step hydrothermal method, the morphologies of the two kinds of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the two kinds of CDs were quasi-spherical with good dispersion, showing that the average particle sizes of the B-CDs and Y-CDs were about 10.2 nm and 7.2 nm, respectively (Figure S1a, b). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images showed that the two kinds of CDs all had lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the interplanar spacing of graphene (100 facet).[43] A typical Raman spectrum (Figure S2a, b) further verified that B-CDs and Y-CDs have disordered surfaces and sp2 conurbation. The survey of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Figure S2c) indicated that the B-CD and Y-CDs consisted of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. The high-resolution XPS spectra (Figure S3) collectively support the formation of CC, CO, C−N, N − H, and C−O−C bonds. The FTIR spectra of B-CDs ( Fig. 1a) showed the presence of characteristic peaks of stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H bonds at 3414 cm−1. peaks at 2951 cm−1, 2839 cm−1 and 1473 cm−1 suggested the presence of -CH2 groups.[44] Stretching signals at 1651 cm−1 suggested the presence of the CO group and the peak at 1554 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and C-H bonds. The peak at 1118 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N bonds [45,46]. The peak at 671 cm−1 was attributed to the out-of-plane bending vibration of O-H. The FTIR spectra of Y-CDs (Fig. 1b) showed the presence of O-H, and N-H bond stretching vibrations at 3337 cm−1. The stretching signals at 1639 cm−1 and 1500 cm−1 suggested the presence of CC, and the characteristic absorption peak at 1384 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-N bond. The characteristic peak at 1211 cm−1 was attributed to C-O bond stretching vibrations. The peak at 748 cm−1 indicated the presence of C-H bonds. The above results indicated the presence of a large number of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of the two kinds of CDs, which enhanced their water solubility. (Figure S4), which indicated that the CDs had a more homogeneous particle size distribution and fewer surface defects.[47,48]

Meanwhile, the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1e, f) also proved that the two kinds of CDs had excitation-independent fluorescence properties. The B-CDs and Y-CDs maintained stable fluorescence emission even in high-salt and highly oxidizing solutions, as well as continuous irradiation

with a xenon lamp for 60 min, suggesting that the two kinds of CDs had good photostability and can be used as excellent nano- fluorescent probes for the construction of sensor. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs and Y-CDs varied significantly in a wide pH range and showed an opposite trend of fluorescence intensity change (Figure S5). This demonstrated that the subsequent construction of the sensor should involve pH optimization to achieve the purpose of differentiating between different heavy metal ions while stabilizing the fluorescence emission intensity of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and optimization of carbon dot/TAPP fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate that CDs and TAPP can be successfully combined, we investigated the changes in fluorescence spectra, absorption spectra, and zeta potential of CDs, TAPP, and CDs/TAPP. The fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased significantly with the addition of different concentrations of TAPP, and the higher the concentration of TAPP, the more obvious the decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs, which indicated that TAPP could burst the fluorescence of CDs. (Figure S6a). As shown in Fig. 2a, the changes in absorption spectra showed that CDs, TAPP, and CDs/TAPP possessed the UV absorption characteristics of both components. In the meanwhile, CDs/TAPP had fluorescence emission peaks at 459 nm, 568 nm, and 650 nm, and the three peaks were separated from each other (Fig. 2b), indicating that the CDs/TAPP complex had been successfully prepared at this time. In addition, the contour plots of CDs and CDs/TAPP also showed significant differences, which was consistent with the results of the 2D fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 2c, d). The zeta potentials of CDs, TAPP, and CDs/TAPP were 2.83, −1.04, and 2.07 mV, respectively (Figure S7). The above results indicated that CDs and TAPP had successfully formed the aggregates and proved the successful construction of the fluorescence sensor. What’s more, to optimize the performance of the sensor and improve the differentiation and sensitivity of the sensor, pH and the concentration of TAPP were optimized. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs/TAPP sensor showed a tendency to increase and then decrease with the increase of TAPP concentration, and reached a plateau at 6 μM (Figure S6b), so according to the change of fluorescence intensity of CDs after adding different concentrations of TAPP,

the optimal concentration of TAPP was finally determined to be 6 μM when the reaction system reached a stable state. Next, for the influence of buffer pH on the stability of heavy metal ions and the fluorescence intensity of CDs, two different pH environments (4.00, 6.09) were selected to optimize the pH value of the sensor. The results of cluster analysis showed that the fluorescence signal changes data under relatively acidic (pH 4.00) and weakly acidic (pH 6.09) conditions were independently distributed (Fig. 2e, f). Meanwhile, to quantify the optimization effect of pH, we further used the Rand Index (RI), Adjusted Rand Index (ARI), and Fowlkes-Mallows Index (FMI) of cluster analysis to evaluate the effects of three different buffer systems on the effect of cluster analysis. The values of the three indicators were 1 for pH 4.00 and 6.09. Therefore, both acidic and near-neutral conditions can be chosen for cluster analysis of nine kinds of heavy metal ions. Finally, considering the trend of fluorescence intensity of the CDs with pH and the requirements of application scenarios, 6.09 was chosen as the optimal pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-sensing of nine heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we can intuitively see that the contour plot of Ni2+ was significantly different when the concentration was 10 μM ( Fig. 3a). The results were similar for the other eight kinds of heavy metal ions (Figure S8). Subsequently, we investigated the ability of the sensor to differentiate nine kinds of heavy metal ions at four concentrations of 50, 100, 200, and 500 μM. The results showed that there were also significant differences in the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of Ni2+ at concentrations of 50 μM, 100 μM and, 200 μM (Fig. 3b–d). The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the other heavy metal ions under the four concentration conditions mentioned above also showed significant differences (Figure S9–12). The above results demonstrated that the fluorescent sensor can successfully distinguish nine kinds of heavy metal ions under five concentrations with good reproducibility and stability regardless of the concentrations. In other words, the multi-emission fluorescence sensor can realize multi-sensing of nine metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-sensing of binary heavy metal ion mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of sensor for detecting binary mixed heavy metal ions under different categories and concentrations conditions was further investigated to cope with the complexity of environmental samples. Firstly, the applicability of sensor to distinguish binary mixed heavy metal categories was investigated (Pb2+/Cd2+, Cd2+/As3+, As3+/Cr3+, and Cr3+/Pb2+). The results indicated that the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the four categories of binary mixed heavy metal ions were significantly different (Fig. 3e, S13). The ability of the sensor to distinguish binary heavy metal mixtures with different concentrations was further examined (1:4, 2:3, 3:2, 4:1). The results showed that the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the mixed heavy metal ions under all four ratios differed significantly (Fig. 3f, S14). This indicated that the sensor could distinguish binary mixed heavy metal ions under different concentration conditions. The above results sufficiently proved that the fluorescence sensor can realize the simultaneous multi-sensing detection of binary mixture samples, which can meet the requirements of complex and variable practical detection scenarios.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Machine learning model building and optimization
ce:section.ce:para: The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra that we collected were complex and variable, making it difficult to directly predict unknown data. Therefore, we utilized the data analysis capabilities of ML to

identify intrinsic correlations between changes in response fluorescence signals and the category and concentration of metal ions. Since the data can be input in two ways, the effects of the two input methods on the prediction performance of the learning model were compared. The first approach was image recognition, directly inputting the image of fingerprint spectra. The accuracy of the predicted categories and concentrations of heavy metal ions was 75.1 % and 64.4 %, respectively (Figure S15). The second approach was spectra data recognition, and the results showed that the accuracy of the predicted categories and concentrations was 97.8 % and 100 %, respectively ( Fig. 4b, c). It was obvious that the performance of fingerprint spectra data recognition was much better. The reason for such a large difference in prediction performance may be that fluorescent fingerprint mapping data mainly contains color intensity information, and deep learning models can easily extract overly complex and redundant features when processing data, leading to the overfitting problem. More importantly, the relatively limited amount of information in the fluorescent fingerprint mapping data makes it difficult to adequately adjust the values of a large number of parameters in the deep learning model, which further increases the difficulty of addressing the overfitting challenge.

As shown in Fig. 4a for the process of building and optimizing the ML model, Firstly, the datasets were divided into training and test sets in the ratio of 8:2. The training set was used for the learning training of the ML model, and the test set verified the prediction performance of the ML model. Next, the performance of five learning models was compared. The results showed that the RF model outperformed the other models in predicting the heavy metal ion categories under the conditions that R 2 and accuracy were maximized and MAE and MSE were minimized (Figure S16). Subsequently, the four metrics were used to assess the performance of the model in predicting heavy metal ion concentrations. The RF model outperformed the other models in predicting heavy metal ion concentrations when R 2 values and accuracy were the highest and MSE and MAE were the lowest. (Figure S17). Based on the above results, it was clear that the RF model can be used as the best learning model for predicting heavy metal ion categories and concentrations. This may be due to the fact that, compared to deep learning, random forests are able to reduce the risk of overfitting, increase model diversity, and perform adaptive selection of features to improve the generalization of the model by means of integrative learning with fewer samples. This makes RF show good performance on small sample learning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Categories and concentrations prediction of heavy metal ions by machine learning models
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the RF model in predicting heavy metal ion categories was first examined. The results suggested that the true metal ion categories were generally consistent with the model predictions within the concentration range of the experiment, and the accuracy could reach 97.8 % on average (Fig. 4b), which indicated that the experimental and model prediction values could be consistent within the error range. Furthermore, the experimental results for predicting heavy metal ion concentrations

showed that the RF model predicted an accuracy of up to 100 % on average (Fig. 4c). All of these can demonstrate that the RF model has excellent prediction performance. The predictive performance of the RF model in binary mixtures was further investigated. The experimental results showed that the accuracy of RF model prediction was 100 % on average when fixing the concentration of heavy metal ions in binary mixtures (100 μM) and varying the mixing categories (Fig. 4d). When the mixing categories were fixed and the proportion of mixed heavy metal ions was varied, the average accuracy of RF model prediction was 91.8 % (Fig. 4e). The above results proved that the RF model had good prediction ability, this may be due to the fact that three-dimensional fluorescence spectra may yield accurate predictions even with small amounts of data with the assistance of ML models and the performance of the model would be further improved with the increase of data volume in the future.

River and tap water were selected as practical samples to investigate the stability and applicability of the method for the detection of heavy metals in complex samples. The experimental results showed that the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the sensor were altered by the addition of single heavy metal ions and binary mixed heavy metal ions to the river and tap water, and the degree of change of the nine kinds of heavy metal ions and binary mixed heavy metal ions varied significantly (Figure S18–S23). This indicated that the method can accurately identify single heavy metal ions and binary mixtures of heavy metals in real samples. Based on the above results, the multi-emitting fluorescent sensor constructed by self-assembly of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and TAPP can be an effective means to identify and distinguish different single and binary mixtures of heavy metal ions, which had the advantages of no need for complex chemical modification and low preparation cost.

The performance of the RF model in predicting heavy metal ions in real samples was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 5a, river and tap water were selected to explore the applicability of RF model. The results suggested that the true metal ion categories were consistent with the model predictions, and the average accuracy could reach 94.4 % (Fig. 5b). What’s more, the performance of the RF model in binary mixtures was examined. The experimental results showed that the accuracy of the RF model in predicting the categories and concentrations of binary mixtures was 100 % and 91.8 %, respectively (Fig. 5c, d). The above results demonstrated that the RF model can accurately predict and differentiate the categories of single and binary mixed heavy metal ions in the real sample river and tap water, showing accurate classification and good prediction ability, and the complex detection environment did not affect the prediction accuracy of the learning model.

In this work, we used ML strategy combined with three-dimensional fluorescence spectra to realize the multi-sensing of nine heavy metals. The three-dimensional fluorescence spectra were selected as the independent sensing output channels to construct the fluorescence sensor, which can produce different fluorescence response signals when nine kinds of different heavy metal ions were added. After collecting the signals and building up the data set, the differentiation and detection of metal ion categories and concentrations can be achieved. Subsequently, the data input way was optimized, and the results showed that the accuracy of image recognition predicted categories and concentrations of heavy metal ions was 75.1 % and 64.4 %, respectively, and the accuracy of data recognition was 97.8 % and 100 %. The results demonstrated that, compared to deep learning, the random forest model processed the data efficiently, capturing subtle changes and feature information in the data to improve the generalization performance of the model. After optimizing the learning model, the RF model can predict the categories and concentrations of a binary heavy metal mixture with accuracies of 100 % and 91.8 % respectively. The prediction accuracy of the analysis method can be significantly improved by optimizing the learning model and the data input method. Finally, the RF model was able to predict single and mixed metal ions categories and concentrations in real sample river and tap water with an accuracy of 94.4 % and 100 %, respectively. This showed that the complex detection environment did not affect the accuracy of the model. The above results demonstrated that the method of using three-dimensional fluorescence signals combined with ML to process the signals can overcome the problems of low differentiation and information richness brought by overlapping and low variability of signals, and solve the problem of preparing multiple sensing units. It also solved the difficulty of preparing multiple sensing units and the problem of not being able to distinguish and identify the metal ion species in unknown samples. It was worth noting that the sensor was easy and cost-effective to construct because it did not involve the tedious synthesis of multiple sensing channels.
None

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 22274134, 21705132, 22376169) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing, China (CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1519).

Liu Hui: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Zhou Jun: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Huang Chengzhi: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Liu Qingqing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xiong Xiaolong: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Chen Jiao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Shuai Xinjia: Validation, Formal analysis. Ye Jingru: Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2025.137385.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jiao Chen received her M.S. degree in College of Pharmaceutical Sciences at Southwest University in 2023. Her research is focused on construction of fluorescent nanoprobes and chemical biosensing.
Xiao Long Xiong is currently pursuing the M.S. degree with the School of Computer and Information Science at Southwest University. His current interest is semi-supervised object detection.
Jing Ru Ye is currently a graduate student at Southwest University. Her research mainly focuses on carbon quantum dots and their applications in chemical analysis and pharmaceutical analysis.

Xin Jia Shuai obtained an undergraduate degree in 2023 in College of Pharmaceutical Sciences at Southwest University in China. Her research work focuses on the development of DNA assemblies based on rolling circle amplification (RCA) technology for microRNA imaging in cancer cells.

Jun Zhou received the Ph.D. degree from University of Electronic Science and Technology of China in 2013. He is currently a Professor with the School of Computer and Information Science at Southwest University. He is the author of more than 50 scientific papers. His current research focuses on machine learning.

Qingqing Liu received her Ph.D. from the University of Alberta in 2016 and is currently a professor at the College of Resources and Environment, Southwest University. Her main research areas are arsenic speciation analysis and environmental toxicology.

Chengzhi Huang is a Ph.D. holder and doctoral supervisor specializing in pharmaceutical analysis. He is a recipient of the National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars, a selected candidate of the National New Century Talents Project, and a Chongqing Talents Program Outstanding Scientist. His research focuses on drug spectral analysis, including biocomputing, intelligent drug analysis, energy transfer spectral analysis, nanoassemblies and bioimaging, and real-time analysis of virus-infected cells.

Hui Liu received her Ph.D. from Southwest University in 2013 and is currently an associate professor at the College of Pharmacy, Southwest University. Her research focuses on biochemical drug analysis, with an emphasis on the preparation of luminescent materials and their applications in drug analysis and disease diagnosis.
